narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it recognized in your profile. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which was the strongest signal pulling toward a higher chance of bar failure, as it placed you near the bottom of your school's performance group late in your academic journey. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 31 carried significant weight in the same direction, suggesting the analytical demands of law school might prove a steep climb. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to this picture, reinforcing early struggles that didn't shift much over time. Even your upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, factored in as tilting toward challenges, perhaps because it didn't buffer against the other hurdles as strongly as we've seen in success stories. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.1 layered on more concern, painting a consistent thread of academic hurdles from undergrad through school. Elements like your white racial background, full-time study plans, and male gender all nudged the prediction further toward that 60% failure risk, weaving together into a profile our model has learned often faces tougher odds on the bar. It's this combination—not any one thing—that tipped the scales, like a story where early stumbles compound without a clear turnaround. You're clearly driven, with full-time commitment ahead, and this isn't the end of your story; many pivot from here by targeting those deciles or LSAT prep. We truly wish you the best as you chart your next steps.
